_: Staged process for publishing this bsp-mcp deployment to MCP discovery registries. Two distinct surfaces — the official MCP Registry (registry.modelcontextprotocol.io, self-service metadata) and the Anthropic Connectors Directory (manual review, ~2-week queue) — with very different content bars and risk profiles. Three stages: substrate hygiene (do now, independent of either registry), MCP Registry listing (do at L1 kernel v2 freeze), Anthropic Directory submission (do 1-2 months after Stage 2 lives without incident). Each stage is reversible until the actual publish/submit; until then, every artifact lives in the repo where it can be revised. Branches 1-3 are the three stages, 4 the risks that submission copy must defuse, 5 the out-of-scope boundary, 6 the recipe/state split that lets other operators reuse this without inheriting one deployment's state, 7 the decisions log, 8 the artifacts shipped in the repo, 9 the current state of the canonical bsp.hermitcrab.me deployment.

1._: Stage 1 — substrate hygiene. Add the things every registry needs regardless of submission timing — LICENSE, accurate README, tool annotations, version alignment. None of this commits to a registry; all of it is owed independently. The audit pattern is: read the spec, fact-check against actual code, write what the code actually does (not what the spec assumes).
1.1: Action: add LICENSE at repo root. MIT matches pscale-commons org convention (4 of 10 repos there are MIT-licensed; the rest are unlicensed). Copyright holder is 'pscale-commons contributors' rather than a single name, matching the org-scoped repo location.
1.2: Action: rewrite README sections that misdescribe the substrate. The pscale-mcp era was Supabase-backed; bsp-mcp is federated-beaches-only (src/db.ts:1-7). Any 'Supabase project', 'SUPABASE_ANON_KEY', or 'Same blocks as pscale-mcp' reference is stale and misleads any registry reviewer. While there, ensure 3+ working usage examples — the Anthropic Directory's minimum.
1.3: Action: add MCP tool annotations to every tool registration in src/server.ts. Missing annotations are the single largest cause of Anthropic Directory rejection (~30%). Use the SDK's 5-arg .tool(name, description, schema, annotations, handler) overload. Apply: readOnlyHint:true on pure-read tools (pscale_verify_rider, pscale_invite); destructiveHint:true on tools that may overwrite (bsp with content/new_lock; pscale_key_publish on rotation); openWorldHint:true on every tool that makes outbound HTTP to federated beaches; title on every tool (Anthropic requires it). The 'same tool reads and writes' case (bsp) is annotated destructive — the honest choice when the surface cannot be split.
1.4: Action: align the version string in src/server.ts McpServer constructor with package.json. A version mismatch in the announced server metadata is a smell registry reviewers notice.
1.5: Validation: the repo passes the audit. LICENSE present; README claims match code; tool annotations declared per MCP spec; version aligned. Smoke tests still pass (npm run smoke:unit, smoke:parser, smoke:sentinel).

2._: Stage 2 — MCP Registry listing at registry.modelcontextprotocol.io. Self-service metadata catalog; no manual review beyond namespace/package verification. Land this after L1 kernel v2 is tagged frozen — before the freeze, the wire is moving and the registry entry would falsely promise stability. The registry uses GitHub OAuth (or DNS auth for custom-domain namespaces); the namespace must match the publishing GitHub identity.
2.1: Action: choose namespace. For pscale-commons org publishing, the namespace is 'io.github.pscale-commons/bsp-mcp'. The GitHub identity authenticating via mcp-publisher must own that org. Alternative under happyseaurchin's personal namespace: 'io.github.happyseaurchin/bsp-mcp' — picks up Stage 3 attribution to a person rather than the org.
2.2: Action: create or update server.json (already drafted at repo root). Shape for remote-only servers: top-level 'name', 'description', 'repository', 'version', plus a 'remotes' block with transport 'streamable-http' and the URL of the canonical deployment. No 'packages' block is required for remote-only servers (no npm publish needed).
2.3: Action: install mcp-publisher and authenticate. brew install mcp-publisher (or grab the binary from the registry's GitHub releases). Then 'mcp-publisher login github' opens a device-auth flow tied to the GitHub account that owns the namespace.
2.4: Action: dry-run then publish. 'mcp-publisher publish --dry-run' validates the manifest; on success, 'mcp-publisher publish' commits the entry. Verification: 'curl https://registry.modelcontextprotocol.io/v0.1/servers?search=<namespace>' returns the entry's metadata.
2.5: Validation: the listing is searchable from MCP-aware clients (Claude Code, claude-app's search-MCP path, mcp-remote with discovery). Agents not given a hardcoded URL can find bsp-mcp by querying the registry.

3._: Stage 3 — Anthropic Connectors Directory submission. Manual review, ~2-week queue. Two failure modes that are immediate-reject: missing or incomplete privacy policy, missing tool annotations (Stage 1 closed the annotations gap). Submit only the canonical deployed instance (bsp.hermitcrab.me/mcp/v1), not the federated protocol — the protocol has no endpoint and the directory lists endpoints. Wait 1-2 months after Stage 2 lives without incident before submitting; the registry listing acts as a soft public-feedback channel.
3.1: Action: host the privacy policy at a stable URL. The drafted PRIVACY.md in repo root can be served via GitHub raw (https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pscale-commons/bsp-mcp-server/main/PRIVACY.md) or via a hermitcrab.me page. The policy describes what the listed connector does: routes, does not store; sentinel reads are in-memory; federated reads are pass-through over HTTPS; logs are operational-only. The policy is for the listed bsp.hermitcrab.me deployment, not for every federated beach (each beach operator is responsible for their own retention).
3.2: Action: assemble form-ready submission copy from specs/anthropic-directory-submission.md. The spec carries: server name, tagline (≤80 chars), description (~300 chars), three use cases, auth type, transport, read/write summary, category, branding asset placeholders. Two pieces are pending until just before submission — current state of v2 freeze, and refreshed sample tool calls — both flagged as TODOs in the spec.
3.3: Action: David fills the Google Form using the spec's copy plus logo and screenshots (which David supplies separately). The assistant cannot submit the form; that step is interactive and bound to a Google account.
3.4: Action: monitor review status. The Anthropic team typically responds within two weeks; rejection comes with reasons; re-submission is permitted after addressing the reasons.
3.5: Validation: the connector appears in the Anthropic Connectors Directory, searchable from inside Claude. Users can add it via the connector UI without manually entering the MCP URL.

4._: Risks the submission copy must defuse explicitly. A reviewer who scans for keywords without understanding the substrate may flag any of these; the description's job is to make the substantive distinction obvious. Each risk has a one-line framing that turns the surface-pattern-match into a substantive non-issue.
4.1: Risk — pscale_verify_rider's sha256 chain + credit conservation reads as crypto/asset-transfer to a quick-scan reviewer. The Anthropic Software Directory Policy explicitly bans 'transfers of money, cryptocurrency, or assets'. Defuse by framing riders as attestations about cooperative work (peer evaluation arithmetic, not value transfer); ecosquared credits are non-fungible per-context tokens; no exchange, no market.
4.2: Risk — URL agent_id federation means the listed connector dispatches outbound HTTP to arbitrary domains. Defuse by declaring openWorldHint:true on every dispatching tool (Stage 1 closed this) and naming the bounded surface in submission copy: the listed connector routes; the publisher is responsible for the routing logic, not for content at federated origins.
4.3: Risk — beaches are open-by-default; anonymous marks are accepted as signal. Defuse by declaring the policy explicitly (open by default; locks are opt-in; tide-clearing handles ephemerality) and pointing out that the listed deployment's policy is its own — each beach operator authors their own.
4.4: Risk — passphrase-based per-block locks instead of OAuth. Defuse by clarifying the auth model: the MCP endpoint itself is open (no client auth); per-block writes carry the passphrase as 'secret' which the federated beach hashes and verifies. This is the substrate's chosen identity model; mismatching with OAuth-bearing connectors does not violate any policy but should be explained.

5._: Boundary: what the listed connector does not cover, made explicit so a reviewer (or a user) does not infer reach the publisher has not claimed. The listed connector is one node in a federated substrate; everything beyond the canonical surface is somebody else's policy.
5.1: Out of scope: operator-deployed beaches that are not bsp.hermitcrab.me's default. Each operator authors their beach's conventions, retention, moderation. The listed connector routes to those origins but does not vouch for them.
5.2: Out of scope: content posted at any beach beyond the publisher's direct control. The publisher controls bsp.hermitcrab.me (the router) and beach.happyseaurchin.com (the default beach). Content at other federated beaches is the operator's responsibility.
5.3: Out of scope: future bsp-mcp deployments operated by third parties. Anyone may clone and deploy bsp-mcp; only the canonical deployment is listed. A separate listing per deployment is the right shape if other operators want directory visibility.

6._: Recipe/state split: branches 1-5 are the universal recipe (any bsp-mcp deployment could follow them to list itself); branch 9 is the per-deployment state (specific to bsp.hermitcrab.me). Other operators forking this repo edit branch 9 for their own deployment without touching the recipe.
6.1: What is universal: the staged method (substrate hygiene → MCP Registry → Anthropic Directory), the risk framings (branch 4), the out-of-scope boundary (branch 5), the artifacts shipped (branch 8). These travel with the code.
6.2: What is per-deployment: branch 9's state, the GitHub namespace in Stage 2.1, the privacy-policy URL in Stage 3.1, the canonical deployment URL throughout. Operators substitute their own.

7._: Decisions log — why this process is staged this way. Each decision has a 'why' that future-self (or another operator inheriting this) can re-evaluate against fresh information without re-deriving from the spec.
7.1: Decision: two surfaces are kept separate. The MCP Registry and the Anthropic Directory have different content bars (self-service catalog vs. manual review with content policy), different audiences (any MCP-aware client vs. Claude users), and different rejection costs (none vs. a written record at Anthropic). Bundling them into one submission compresses a multi-week review into one decision.
7.2: Decision: MCP Registry first, after v2 freeze. The freeze is what makes 'this wire is stable' a truthful claim. Listing before the freeze would point agents at a moving target. The catalog has no content gatekeeping beyond namespace verification, so timing is the only real consideration.
7.3: Decision: Anthropic Directory deferred 1-2 months past Stage 2. The registry listing acts as a soft public feedback channel; bugs surface there cheaply. Submitting to the Directory while bugs are still common burns review cycles. Also: the Directory submission is a description-and-review exercise as much as a code one, and the description benefits from the substrate having lived in the wild.
7.4: Decision: MIT license. Matches the pscale-commons org convention on existing licensed repos (pscale, agents, bleeding-edge, seed-spore). Apache-2.0 considered for the explicit patent grant (relevant for a federated substrate with multiple implementations) and shelved as overkill for this codebase's surface area.

8._: Artifacts shipped in this repo. The block describes the process; the files implement it. Edit-and-track happens in the files; this block updates branch 9 when stages complete.
8.1: LICENSE at repo root — Stage 1.1. MIT, pscale-commons contributors as copyright holder.
8.2: README.md at repo root — Stage 1.2. The Usage examples section covers the Anthropic Directory minimum (three working examples); the Substrate section describes the federated reality (Stage 1.2 closed the Supabase claim).
8.3: server.json at repo root — Stage 2.2. Remote-server shape with streamable-http transport pointing at https://bsp.hermitcrab.me/mcp/v1. Validated locally; not yet published.
8.4: PRIVACY.md at repo root — Stage 3.1. Describes what the listed connector does (routes, in-memory sentinel reads, pass-through federated HTTP); does not speak for federated beach operators.
8.5: specs/anthropic-directory-submission.md — Stage 3.2. Form-ready copy with TODOs flagged where content depends on v2-freeze state at submission time.

9._: Current submission state for the canonical bsp.hermitcrab.me deployment as of 2026-05-16. Stage 1 complete in this commit; Stage 2 prepared and gated on L1 kernel v2 freeze; Stage 3 prepared and gated on Stage 2 plus 1-2 months of clean operation. Each branch advances as work lands; update at every stage transition.
9.1: Stage 1 — complete as of 2026-05-16. LICENSE added; README updated; tool annotations in server.ts for all 7 tools; version aligned 0.2.0 → 0.4.0 to match package.json; this directory block bundled.
9.2: Stage 2 — prepared, gated on L1 kernel v2 freeze. server.json drafted at repo root. To execute: choose namespace, run mcp-publisher publish from a machine authenticated to the GitHub identity that owns the namespace.
9.3: Stage 3 — prepared, gated on Stage 2 plus 1-2 months. PRIVACY.md drafted at repo root; specs/anthropic-directory-submission.md carries the form-ready copy with TODOs for the v2-freeze-time refresh. To execute: David fills the Google Form using the spec's copy plus logo/screenshots.
